coredata.dc:title: Luminescent carbon dots with concentration-dependent emission in solution and yellow emission in solid state
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Ba, Xiao-Xu
Zhang, Lu
Yin, Yu-Lin
Jiang, Feng-Lei
Jiang, Peng
Liu, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) is a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with favorable stability and optical properties. However, CDs in solid state often suffer from fluorescence quenching due to π-π stacking of conjugated systems, like most small molecules or organic dyes. Herein, we prepared the CDs that generate bright yellow luminescence in solid state without any additional matrix, meanwhile the solid CDs are inclined to assembly into spherical structure. While the CDs are dissolved in aqueous solution, the photoluminescence (PL) emission from blue to green can be realized by regulating the concentration of CDs. With the concentration increasing, the self-assembly behavior of CDs is observed in solution, which leads to the bathochromic shift of photoluminescence. Besides, the mechanism of PL conversion in this process was proposed based on the characterization results, that with the concentration of CDs rising in solution, π-π interaction was restrained while electron redistribution was induced. Consequently, a localized state II caused by electron rearrangement gradually becomes the predominant emission state, resulting in the PL emission shifting to long-wavelength region. Moreover, CDs have shown favorable potentials in the field of anti-counterfeit and multicolor bioimaging, making the CDs highly attractive for a wide variety of applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solid photoluminescence
Aggregation
Bathochromic shift
Self-assembly
Mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization results of CDs. (a) TEM image of CDs. (b) The size distribution of CDs. (c) XRD spectrum of CDs. (d) Photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum and emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths of CDs. (e) XPS spectrum of CDs. (f) FTIR spectrum of CDs.

Photoluminescence spectra of CDs in solid state. (a) Excitation (black line) and emission (red line) scan of solid CDs, the inset is solid CDs under room light condition (left) and 365 nm UV illumination (right). (b) Emission spectra of solid CDs under different excitation wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Photoluminescence spectra of CDs solution in different concentrations. (a-c) PLE and emission spectra of 0.25 mg/mL (a), 1 mg/mL (b), 5 mg/mL (c) CDs solution. (d) Normalized PL spectra of 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2.5, 5, 10 mg/mL CDs solution under corresponding maximum excitation wavelength.
TEM images of 0.25 mg/mL (a) and 1 mg/mL (b) CDs solution. (Insets in top right and bottom right corner are the statistic size distribution and HRTEM images of CDs, respectively).
TEM image of 5 mg/mL CDs. (The pictures below are the enlarged part of the white circle).
The variation trend of zeta potential in different concentrations CDs.

Confocal images of Hela cells incubated with 50 μg/mL CDs solution for 4 h. (a) and (d) are the images of Hela cells under bright field; (b) and (d) are the fluorescent images with excitation at 405 nm and 488 nm, respectively; (c) and (f) are the merged images of Hela cells incubated with CDs. Scale bar: 36 μm.
Diagram of aggregation process and PL variation for CDs.
The schematic mechanism diagram of fluorescent emission redshift.
Multi-exponential decay curves of CDs solution at 0.25, 1, 5 mg/mL and solid state, respectively.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Herein, τ i represents the corresponding lifetime of different components, B stands for the corresponding pre-exponential factor, τ av denotes the average lifetime.

head.ce:title: Luminescent carbon dots with concentration-dependent emission in solution and yellow emission in solid state

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (MOE)
College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) is a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with favorable stability and optical properties. However, CDs in solid state often suffer from fluorescence quenching due to π-π stacking of conjugated systems, like most small molecules or organic dyes. Herein, we prepared the CDs that generate bright yellow luminescence in solid state without any additional matrix, meanwhile the solid CDs are inclined to assembly into spherical structure. While the CDs are dissolved in aqueous solution, the photoluminescence (PL) emission from blue to green can be realized by regulating the concentration of CDs. With the concentration increasing, the self-assembly behavior of CDs is observed in solution, which leads to the bathochromic shift of photoluminescence. Besides, the mechanism of PL conversion in this process was proposed based on the characterization results, that with the concentration of CDs rising in solution, π-π interaction was restrained while electron redistribution was induced. Consequently, a localized state II caused by electron rearrangement gradually becomes the predominant emission state, resulting in the PL emission shifting to long-wavelength region. Moreover, CDs have shown favorable potentials in the field of anti-counterfeit and multicolor bioimaging, making the CDs highly attractive for a wide variety of applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) is the general term for carbon-based spherical nanomaterials with its three-dimensional size below 10 nm [1]. Considerable researches in applications for CDs were explored due to its excellent solubility, stability, biocompatibility and low toxicity, comparing to both organic molecules and metal quantum dots (QDs) [2–5]. In addition, extensive sources of raw materials and diverse synthetic methods also advantage its applications. So far, CDs has been applied to various fields, such as, ions or biomolecule probes [6–9], light emitting devices [10–13], fluorescent cellular imaging [14–16], anti-counterfeiting [17–19], near-infrared photoluminescence [20–23], and visualized tumor therapy [24–26].

In order to realize the diversity in applications, multi-color CDs has been the research hotspot in recent years. There are a variety of methods to achieve the modulation of fluorescence emission, by altering the raw materials, reaction temperature or time to modify the surface morphology or oxidation degree [27–30], also adjusting existential form of nitrogen elements [31], or regulating size or thickness to obtain polychromatic CDs [32,33], and some CDs materials are able to transform PL color by changing the solvent polarity [34–36]. However, it is rare studied that the fluorescence conversion can be achieved uniquely by the concentration enrichment of the same product. Li et al. obtained phosphorus-doped CDs that owns aggregation-caused redshift of emission, while the synthesis procedure requires a long-term reaction and the mechanism of PL redshift was not given clearly [37]. Herein, we synthesized the CDs that owns concentration-dependent PL emission accompanied by self-assembly behavior in solution, also the solid of CDs emits bright yellow fluorescence under ultraviolet light. Moreover, this reaction is economical and efficient, and we make further investigation and discussion on this aggregation phenomenon. Last, the mechanism of PL variation is proposed to explain how the PL is modulated by concentration.

Citric acid (CA) monohydrate, ammonia solution, sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), 3-(4,5-dimethyldiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further treatment. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water from the Millipore water purification system (18.2 MΩ
·
cm−1).

Hydrothermal method was used to synthesize CDs. 0.56 g CA monohydrate was dissolved in deionized water (920 μL) to form a colorless transparent solution, then 80 μL ammonium hydroxide was added, the mix were sealed into a 50 mL autoclaves and placed into an oven heating at 200 °C for 1 h. After the reaction ended and cooled to room temperature, 10 mL deionized water was added, the formed brown suspension was centrifuged (13,000 rpm) for 5 min and then filtered through a 0.22 μm micro-filtration membrane to obtain the liquid supernatant. Then the supernatant was extracted with dichloromethane by a separatory funnelto remove by-products and small organic molecules, the rotary evaporation was used for the supernatant to remove extra dichloromethane, after evaporation, the aqueous solution was dialyzed in a 1,000 Da dialysis bag for 8 h. The CDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying method.

Cell Viability: Hela cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% FBS, 1% penicillin and 1% amphotericin at 37 °C in the atmosphere of 5% CO2. MTT assay was used to test the cell viability of CDs. Hela cells were seeded into a 96-well plate at appropriate density and incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 12 h. For MTT assay, cells were further cultured for 24 h in the presence of CDs with different concentrations (0, 10, 20, 40, 100, 200 μg/mL). Cells cultured without CDs were taken as the control group. After incubation, the cells were washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). Then, 20 μL of 5 mg/mL MTT solution was added to every cell well and incubated at 37 °C. The blank group were added with MTT solution without cells. After 4 h, the culture medium with MTT was removed, 150 μL of DMSO was added, followed by vibrating for 15 min to dissolve the purple precipitates. Finally, the optical density (OD) of the mixture at 490 nm was measured on an Elax800 Microplate reader (BioTek).
C e l l
V i a b i l i t y
( % )
=
OD
samples
-
OD
blank
OD
control
-
OD
blank
× 100 %

Cellular Imaging: For cellular imaging, after an incubation of 4 of Hela cells with CDs solution, with a concentration of 50 μg/mL in the culture medium. Prior to fixation of the cells on the slide for inspection with a confocal fluorescence microscope, the cells were washed with PBS buffer three times to remove the excess CDs.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were collected on Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA), ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were measured on Cary Series UV-Vis Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). All the time-resolved spectra were obtained on the Edinburgh FLS-1000 PL spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK), and absolute quantum yield (QY) results were acquired using an integrating sphere connected by FLS1000 apparatus (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were recorded on a JEOL JEM-2100 (HR) electron microscope. Infrared spectra (IR) were operated on the NICOLET 5700 FTIR Spectrometer (Thermo, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum were measured on X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical B.V., Holland). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to record the element analysis results. Zeta potential was measured by ZETA-sizer, Nano-ZS90 (Malvern Instruments, UK). The MTT assay was conducted on a BioTek microplate reader. Cellular imaging was performed with a Perkin-Elmer Ultraview Vox confocal microscope (MA). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) image of solid samples was recorded on the FEI Verios460 microscope. (FEI, USA).

The as-synthesized CDs was characterized via TEM, PL spectrophotometer, XRD and XPS means to confirm the structure and luminescent nature of carbon nanoparticles. As is illustrated in Fig. 1 a, CDs synthesized via hydrothermal method possess favorable dispersity in aqueous solution, TEM image of CDs presents spherical particles and the average diameter of CDs is 2.46 nm (Fig. 1b). The inset indicates the crystalline lattice space is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (1 0 0) facet of graphitic carbon [38]. The two main peaks around 23° and 43° of XRD spectrum in Fig. 1c are attributed to the interlayer spacing of 0.34 nm and 0.21 nm, respectively [39]. The PL spectra in Fig. 1d and three-dimensional (3D) contour fluorescent spectra of CDs solution (Fig. S1) demonstrates the maximum emission wavelength is 440 nm when CDs was excited at 360 nm, and apparently it possesses the property of PL excited-dependence. These characterization results verify the formation of highly-crystallographic, uniformly-distributed, and blue-emitting CDs.

In addition, the stability of CDs under various conditions was investigated (Fig. S2). It reveals that the luminescence intensity was hardly affected even at high salt concentration or excited for 100 times (Fig. S2a, S2b). During the pH adjusted from 1 to 14, it can be seen that the CDs solution is relatively stable in acidic environment, but PL intensity gradually decreases when pH exceeds 12, which is related to the abundant carboxyl groups contained on CDs (Fig. S2c). And the fluorescence intensity decreased only by 9% after two hours of excitation (Fig. S2d). With the temperature varied from 283 K to 333 K, the emission intensity drops by 25% (Fig. S2e). While the PL intensity is almost no change after the CDs solution was placed for 40 days (Fig. S2f). Overall, CDs possesses favorable photobleaching resistance and chemical durability.

XPS and IR spectroscopy can be utilized to analyze the chemical bonds and functional groups that may exist in CDs. XPS spectrum in Fig. 1e shows three main peaks at 285.1 eV, 400.1 eV, 531.8 eV, representing the binding energy of C1s, N1s and O1s, the atomic ratio of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen are 63.6%, 3.9% and 32.4% respectively. The high-resolved spectrum (Fig. S3a) of C1s band at 288.6, 285.1 and 284.4 eV are assigned to CC/CC, CO/CN and CO, respectively. The peaks of N1s spectra at 400.1 and 399.6 eV indicates the presence of CN and pyridine N (Fig. S3b). Typical O1s peaks at 533.1, 531.8 and 531.2 eV represent for OC, OCOH and OC, respectively [40] (Fig. S3c). FTIR spectrum of CDs in Fig. 1f displays these predominant bands around 3453, 2935, 1700, 1650, 1409, 1359, and 1170 cm−1, which are assigned to νO-H, νC=H, νC=O, νC=N, δC-H, νC-N and νC-O respectively [41,42]. Combined with the results of XPS and IR spectra, CDs with hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl and amide groups may have formed during the carbonization process, which are attributed to the hydrophilic groups from raw materials of CA and ammonium hydroxide.

Aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) generally occurs on the solid state of common CDs due to excessive Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) or direct π-π stacking interactions, which is similar to organic molecules [43,44]. Nevertheless, solid CDs prepared by this convenient hydrothermal method emits bright yellow luminescence under ultraviolet lamp (The inset in Fig. 2 a). The maximum excitation and emission wavelength of solid CDs are 470 and 560 nm, respectively (Fig. 2a). The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) measured by integrating sphere was about 16%. Emission spectra of solid CDs in Fig. 2b under different excitation wavelength were recorded, from 320 to 440 nm, the maximum emission wavelength of solid is barely changed, distinct form the emission spectra of CDs in aqueous solution (Fig. 1d). It’s generally accepted that π-π stacking interaction in solid aggregate states may result in the quenching of fluorescence [32,44,45], most practice of preventing ACQ is to avoid the intensive contact between CDs and spread them out, for example, dispersing CDs into solid matrix or introducing polymer chains in to CDs [5,46,47]. However, CDs solid synthesized in our work does not require the incorporation of other matrices to illuminate. Besides, compared to the PLQY of 8.2% and blue emission of CDs in dilute solution, solid CDs has not only a higher QY but also a bathochromic-shift in luminescence. When CDs are dispersed in solution, the efficiency that energy converting to fluorescence may decrease due to the motion of molecules or the collision with solvent molecules, leading to the decline of QY. Meanwhile, the regular arrangement of monomers in solid phase results in the expansion of π-π conjugated area, so the energy band of solid emission becomes narrower, which brings on the yellow luminescence of solid CDs [48,49].

In the study of PL properties for CDs, we found an interesting phenomenon: the maximum emission region shifted from blue to green as the concentration of CDs increased (Fig. S4). Thus we diluted the lyophilized CDs with deionized water to different concentration gradients and recorded a series of measurement results. Take 0.25, 1 and 5 mg/mL CDs for examples, the maximum emission wavelengths are 430, 450 and 520 nm, respectively (Fig. 3 a–c), and PL spectra reveals that they are all excitation wavelength-dependent emission (Fig. S5a–c). Also, the 3D PL spectra of 1 mg/mL CDs solution (Fig. S5b) indicates that the emission center gradually shifts to the region of long wavelength. When the concentration changed from 0.1 mg/mL to 10 mg/mL, the maximum emission wavelength transfers from 430 to 535 nm as shown in Fig. 3d. Meanwhile, the UV-Vis spectra of CDs in low concentrations reveals that their absorption has a tendency to red shift, and the absorption in the longer wavelength region is continuously enhanced (Fig. S6a). Besides, the emission spectra of low concentration solution CDs have a large overlap with the excitation spectra, which may induce the FRET phenomenon. Also, the PLQY of 0.25 mg/mL CDs solution is 8.5%, and it declines as the concentration of CDs continues to increases (Table S1). As the density of CQDs monomers in solution increases, the collision between monomers and solvent molecules becomes more frequent, which may consume part of the energy in the process, thereby reducing the efficiency of conversion to fluorescence, leading to the decrease of QY. Moreover, when the concentration varied from 1 to 2.5 mg/mL, there is a jump both in the excitation and emission wavelength (Fig. S6b), which indicates the assembly behavior of CDs may have transformed into another pattern within this concentration range.

In order to further figure out the behavior of CDs at high concentration, we used TEM to explore the morphology and distribution of CDs under varying concentrations. First, as present in Fig. 4 , CDs of 0.25 and 1 mg/mL are well dispersed in aqueous solution, a distinct crystal spacing of 0.21 nm can be both observed (insets in Fig. 4a and b), the average size is 2.27 and 2.25 nm, respectively. Besides, the size distribution in the two concentrations is nearly the same, as the concentration varied from 0.25 to 1 mg/mL, only the density of particle distribution and the spatial distance between particles changed, no aggregation phenomenon occurs in this process. However, CDs with uniform distribution as shown in Fig. 4 were not observed in the solution of 5 mg/mL, instead only two to three CDs monomers appeared in view under the 50 nm scale (Fig. S7a). Meanwhile, a lot of black blocks were distributed in the field of vision (Fig. S7b), when this part was magnified in Fig. 5 , several clearly ordered lattice of consistent 0.21 nm can be observed. Other than the circled part, there are lattice distribution elsewhere which can also be observed directly in Fig. 5. Compared to the average diameter of 2.3 nm for CDs, the area of the uniform lattice distribution is much larger than the area of a single carbon dot, that verifies the black blocks are the aggregates of CDs, this phenomenon proves that the monomers of CDs do indeed undergo the behavior of self-assembly under dense concentrations. Besides, the lattice arrangement direction of different aggregates that formed by CDs is not consistent, it shows the CDs of high concentration develop a number of self-assemblies with different orientations. To further explore the assembly behavior at dense concentration, we measured the SEM image of CDs in solid state. It is interesting that most monomers of CDs have assembled into spheres of different sizes (Fig. S8), which proves that dense CDs do have the tendency to self-assemble due to intermolecular forces. In addition, due to the varying assembly orientations, solid CDs are prone to form spherical assemblies of diverse dimensions. Therefore, it is inferred that when the density of monomers distribution increases, the interaction between abundant groups on the surface advantages the aggregation behavior of CDs, as a result, the intense contact between CDs induced the self-assembly behavior.

Zeta potential is an important indicator for characterizing the stability of dispersed system. The absolute value of Zeta potential represents the dispersion stability, positive or negative value stands for the charge type carried by particles. The higher the absolute value of the zeta potential predicts a more stable dispersion, conversely, the lower potential, the more prone to aggregate or flocculate. We measured the zeta potential of CDs solution under different concentrations (Table S1), the variation trend is displayed in Fig. 6 . From 0.1 to 1 mg/mL, the absolute value of zeta potential gradually decreases and approaches to zero. However, the tendency starts to go down when the concentration continues to increase beyond 1 mg/mL. This unusual phenomenon demonstrates that the attraction between CDs overtakes the repulsive force when at low concentration, which may induce the aggregation behavior. Nevertheless, when the concentration of CDs solution exceeds 1 mg/mL, the aggregation extent of the CDs gradually becomes saturated, and the excess carbon dots continue to be dispersed in the solution, which makes the integral state is relatively stable, thus the zeta potential gradually becomes more negative at higher concentrations.

Combining the results of PL, TEM and zeta potential experiments at different conditions, a diagram that displays the aggregation process and fluorescence conversion of CDs is present in Scheme 1 . In the dilute solution, the monomers of CDs are equably dispersed and emit blue fluorescence; when the concentration exceeds 1 mg/mL, CDs are inclined to aggregate and form directional and orderly assemblies, accompanied by a pronounced red-shift fluorescence in this state, from blue to green emission; with the concentration continuing to rise, the degree of aggregation approach to saturation yet the green luminescence is still dominant; when CDs reach the anhydrous solid state and assembly into spherical structure, the conjugated π-domains is enlarged and yellow emission is achieved.

To further understand the bathochromic shift of PL under concentrated solution, we used the time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy to record decay curves of CDs at different conditions, the measured average lifetime is basically invariable as the concentration of CDs solution increases (Table S1 and Fig. S9). Take 0.25, 1 and 5 mg/mL of CDs solutions for examples, as shown in Table 1 , the multiple de-excitation processes of CDs solutions fitted well to the triple-exponential function, which can be regarded containing a fast portion about 1 ns and two slow one around 4 ns and 12 ns. However, according to the calculated percentage of each part, the first two components are predominant during the process of fluorescence decay. Generally, the fast bandgap transition and the slow one of CDs represent the luminescence dominated by core region and surface state, respectively [50]. Meanwhile, it is remarkable that as the concentration of CDs solution increases, the proportion of first part is decreasing while the second one is rising. So, it can be inferred that the PL emission of CDs is predominant by two states: state I of the fast transition between energy levels, induced by sp2-domain carbon zone with less defects; state II related to long-decay emission which is caused by the hybridization of carbon backbone or oxygen-related functional groups [51,52]. Besides, in the process of concentration increasing, stats II gradually dominates the fluorescence emission of CDs solution. However, the emission state of solid CDs is constituted by two components. Of which, the short one is the dominant part, and it is attributed to the intrinsic state of sp2 hybridization region. When CDs is in solid phase, the monomers are rearranged into spheres and this regular permutation expands the π-π conjugated region, resulting in the yellow luminescence.

As illustrated in Scheme 2 , we proposed a mechanism to explain the luminescence property of CDs. When the electrons in ground state absorb photons and transit to the excited state, they may shift to another lower vibrational level of excited state via internal conversion and vibration relaxation, then fluorescence is released when the electrons return to the ground state. The dispersed CDs emit blue emission that could be ascribed to the transition between π-π* gap of intrinsic state, the state I that controlled by the graphitic domains of sp2 carbon clusters. While the high density of CDs in solution that shorten the distance between surface groups may induce the electron distribution, and plenty carboxyl that exist in the surface of CDs are reported to be able to restrain π-π interactions and initiate distortion in conjugated system [8]. Thus a localized state II caused by electron rearrangement and distortion gradually becomes the predominant emission state [53]. The energy gap between highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of state II is more narrow than the state I, as a result, the electronic transitions from state II cause a broader absorption and PL shift to long-wavelength emission region [54]. As for the solid CDs, vast monomers of CDs regularly pack into spherical structure and the sp2 conjugated area is enlarged, the energy band between excited state and ground state becomes narrower, leading to the bright yellow luminescence of solid CDs.

Due to the unique fluorescent properties of CDs, they have shown to be feasible in the application of fluorescent ink and cellular imaging. CDs powder were dispersed in ethanol-aqua solvent and the solution was directly utilized to write on a filter paper, the words can only be observed under the UV lamp while no visible signs can be identified under daylight (Fig. S10). Such conversion feature could potentially be used in anti-counterfeit applications. Also, CDs are verified to possess favorable biocompatibility and are capable to be applied in bioimaging field. The MTT assay shows the cell viability of Hela cells was above 90% even at a concentration of 200 μg/mL CDs (Fig. S11), it demonstrated that CDs possess fine biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Moreover, confocal microscope images exhibit that CDs have successfully penetrated the cell membrane without further bioconjugation (Fig. 7 ). Blue and green fluorescence could be observed in cells when excited at 405 nm and 488 nm respectively. it reveals CDs can be utilized as biolabeling agents under multi-wavelength of laser excitation channel.

In summary, CDs that possesses concentration-dependent luminescence in solution and yellow emission in solid state was synthesized via a facile method. With large amount of carboxyl decorated on the surface, CDs endows excellent dispersibility and stability in aqueous solution. Through the evidence of TEM, Zeta potential and fluorescence lifetime, we found the self-assembly behavior of CDs solution at high concentrations, making the dominant PL emission center convert from π-π interactions of intrinsic state to localized state, which arises from electron redistribution and distortion of oxygen-related groups. The gap between excited state and ground state of predominant localized state may be narrower than that of intrinsic state, resulting in the broadening of absorption band and red-shift of PL emission. Besides, the CDs monomers in solid state are assembled into spheres of diverse sizes, which expands the π-π conjugated area and shorten the energy gap, bringing on the bathochromic yellow fluorescence. The research about fluorescent bathochromic-shift and corresponding mechanism may initiate promising enlightenment for the future synthesis and regulation of carbon nanomaterials. Moreover, CDs are available as fluorescent ink and bioimaging agents, which reveals significant potential for the further application in anti-counterfeit and biological fields.

Xiao-Xu Ba: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Lu Zhang: Software, Investigation. Yu-Lin Yin: Formal analysis. Feng-Lei Jiang: Funding acquisition. Peng Jiang: Resources, Validation. Yi Liu: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Nature Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21873075), Guangxi Scientific and Technological Development Projects (AD17195081), and BAGUI Scholar Program of Guangxi Province of China (2016).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2020.01.006.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
